title: By Jonah Goldberg July 11, 2016 11:14 AM PT Share via Close extra sharing options Email Facebook X LinkedIn Threads Redd
raw_response: {"rating": 1.5, "lean": "Lean Right", "explanation": "This is an opinion piece by a self-identified conservative columnist. The author attempts to address blind spots on both sides of the policing debate, but the overall framing leans right through its critique of liberal contradictions and its emphasis on conservative principles. The balanced structure moderates the score, but the ideological center of gravity is lean right. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author identifies as conservative and frames arguments from that perspective, using phrases like 'Conservatives, of all people, should understand' — positioning himself within the conservative movement while urging it to be more consistent with its own principles. (2) The critique of liberals is more pointed and ideologically loaded than the critique of conservatives. The author characterizes liberals as 'obtuse' in discussing policing and describes 'one of the strangest ironies of modern liberalism' — that they trust government but not police. This frames liberalism as internally contradictory. (3) The author frames the state's 'first obligation' as defending 'safety and property of its citizens' and warns against 'vilifying those sworn to uphold' the law — language that aligns with right-leaning law-and-order values. (4) The author introduces Roland Fryer's research to complicate the narrative that police disproportionately shoot Black people, noting Fryer 'called it the most surprising result I have found in my entire career' and adding 'Fryer, by the way, is African American' — a rhetorical choice that uses the researcher's race to bolster credibility in challenging a left-leaning narrative. (5) The author dismisses Giuliani's 'black-on-black crime' framing, and acknowledges the legitimacy of African American distrust of government, which provides genuine balance and prevents the piece from scoring higher on the right scale. (6) The characterization of 'National conversations' as 'usually efforts to bully everyone into accepting a single narrative' implicitly critiques progressive discourse norms, aligning with right-leaning skepticism of cultural consensus-building."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is an opinion piece by a self-identified conservative columnist. The author attempts to address blind spots on both sides of the policing debate, but the overall framing leans right through its critique of liberal contradictions and its emphasis on conservative principles. The balanced structure moderates the score, but the ideological center of gravity is lean right. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author identifies as conservative and frames arguments from that perspective, using phrases like 'Conservatives, of all people, should understand' — positioning himself within the conservative movement while urging it to be more consistent with its own principles. (2) The critique of liberals is more pointed and ideologically loaded than the critique of conservatives. The author characterizes liberals as 'obtuse' in discussing policing and describes 'one of the strangest ironies of modern liberalism' — that they trust government but not police. This frames liberalism as internally contradictory. (3) The author frames the state's 'first obligation' as defending 'safety and property of its citizens' and warns against 'vilifying those sworn to uphold' the law — language that aligns with right-leaning law-and-order values. (4) The author introduces Roland Fryer's research to complicate the narrative that police disproportionately shoot Black people, noting Fryer 'called it the most surprising result I have found in my entire career' and adding 'Fryer, by the way, is African American' — a rhetorical choice that uses the researcher's race to bolster credibility in challenging a left-leaning narrative. (5) The author dismisses Giuliani's 'black-on-black crime' framing, and acknowledges the legitimacy of African American distrust of government, which provides genuine balance and prevents the piece from scoring higher on the right scale. (6) The characterization of 'National conversations' as 'usually efforts to bully everyone into accepting a single narrative' implicitly critiques progressive discourse norms, aligning with right-leaning skepticism of cultural consensus-building.
